abstract: Assess whether quantum reservoirs (with PCA-encoded inputs) can outperform classical ones and how entanglement changes feature mapping.
artifacts_summary: Updated writing_presentation follow-up output with explicit manuscript artifact typing for paper/main.tex, cleared validation-blocking status, and synchronized tracker/research-trace updates.
constraints: Problem framing is exploratory/open-question and should avoid unsupported quantum-advantage claims.
Target deadline provided as 2026-03-10 (already past at intake time 2026-03-25).
evaluation_plan: Compare quantum vs classical reservoirs on image classification with PCA-component sweeps; run ablations with and without entangling evolution; use fixed train/validation/test splits and report classification metrics plus sensitivity to number of components, measured observables, and reservoir settings; include at least one dataset beyond MNIST if MNIST separability after PCA obscures differences.
full_description:
I want to know if there is a quantum advantage in Quantum Reservoir Computing for image classification. If we take an image, and compute its PCA decomposition, and I feed the first X components to the reservoir as inputs (e.g. angle encoding), is there an advantage to using a quantum reservoir? What is the role of entanglement? How does the reservoir transform the features with and without entanglement? As a reservoir, we can take a simple transverse Ising model and angle encoding, and measuring a subset of pauli operators. You can try the MNIST dataset for digit classification, although I feel that the PCA is already so discriminatory (the only information in the picture is the digit, so *any* substantial difference between pictures immediately translates to a different classificaiton) that it will be hard to improve anything with QRC. Would other datasets be better test cases?

Papers listing to download for review and context:

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.06873
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2409.00998
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.14677
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.08605
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.11020
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.02553
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.06758

Resource-informed context from provided references (workspace/resources): the attached papers include works explicitly on quantum extreme learning/image classification, entanglement and classical simulability, measurement-operator optimization in QRC, and foundations on encoding expressivity and quantum models as kernel methods. This supports positioning the study around advantage conditions, entanglement effects, and readout design choices.
goals: Assess whether a quantum reservoir can outperform a classical reservoir for PCA-encoded image classification under CPU-only constraints.
Isolate and quantify the role of entanglement by comparing feature mappings and performance with and without entangling dynamics.
Evaluate a transverse Ising reservoir with angle encoding and partial Pauli-observable readout on MNIST and potentially harder alternatives.

constraints: Problem framing is exploratory/open-question and should avoid unsupported quantum-advantage claims.
Target deadline provided as 2026-03-10 (already past at intake time 2026-03-25).
